title: Encoder-Only Models
description: Models that read the full input before producing a representation or label, without a separate generative decoder stack.
openingSummary: Encoder-only models matter when the reader needs a model that understands an input all at once instead of generating token by token: the stack can attend bidirectionally across the sequence, build a strong internal representation, then hand that representation to a classification, retrieval, or masked-token head.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: An encoder-only model keeps the transformer-style stack on the reading side only. Tokens enter, the stack updates contextual vectors with full left-and-right visibility, and a task head reads those vectors for classification, retrieval, tagging, or masked-token prediction. There is no separate decoder that emits a new sequence step by step.

howItDiffers.title: How It Differs From Nearby Categories
howItDiffers.body: Compared with decoder-only models, encoder-only models optimize understanding rather than open-ended generation. Compared with encoder-decoder models, they skip the second stack and therefore do not need cross-attention into encoder memory during output generation. They often sit closer to BERT-style retrieval, ranking, or labeling tasks than to chatbot or completion loops.

representativeCheckpoints.title: Representative Checkpoints
representativeCheckpoints.body: BERT is the canonical checkpoint to keep in mind here. The model reads text bidirectionally, then uses heads tuned for tasks such as masked-language modeling, sentence classification, or embedding-style similarity. The linked pages below connect that checkpoint to the encoder concept, transformer family, and masked-language-model category.
